items.title: Kursforum
items.text:
A course-forum web application where students in the TIA, TIS and TIK courses post topics and comment on them. Built with Express 5, Pug and PostgreSQL, fronted by Nginx and run with Docker Compose.

A DHBW Web-Engineering project by Amica Schneider, Tamara Bronner, Stefan Malesevic and Martin Ließ.

Tutorials

Start here. Get the whole stack running and open the forum in your browser.

Quickstart

How-to guides

Task recipes: run with or without Docker, seed demo data, run the tests.

Run with Docker

Reference

The exact facts: HTTP API, environment variables, database schema.

API reference

Explanation

How the pieces fit: architecture, the auth model, the security layers.

Architecture

items.title: Quick links
items.text: Resource Where Source repository Just-Martin-Really/Web-Engineering-Semester1 Run it locally Quickstart API endpoints API reference Configuration Environment variables Report an issue GitHub Issues

items.title: Explanation
items.text:
Background and design context for Kursforum: how a request travels through the stack, how authentication works, and which security layers sit in front of the app and why.

Browse the explanation pages in the sidebar.

items.title: Architecture
items.text:
See also: Database schema · Security · Auth model

Kursforum is a single Express application that both renders HTML pages with Pug and serves a JSON API. It stores everything in PostgreSQL and, in the Docker setup, sits behind an Nginx reverse proxy.

items.title: Request flow
items.text:
flowchart LR
    Browser --> Nginx["Nginx :80"]
    Nginx --> App["Express :3001"]
    App --> PG[("PostgreSQL :5432")]

In development without Docker, Nginx is absent and the browser talks to Express directly on port 3001. Either way, Express is the single entry point for both pages and the API.

items.title: Layers inside the app
items.text:
A request passes through a fixed middleware chain before reaching a route, set up in server.js:

  1. Request ID — tags each request for tracing in the logs.
  2. HTTPS enforcement — redirects to HTTPS when ENFORCE_HTTPS=true.
  3. Security headers — Helmet plus a few custom headers.
  4. CORS — a development or production config depending on NODE_ENV.
  5. Body parsing — JSON only, capped at 10kb.
  6. Session — an Express session backed by the PostgreSQL session table.

Routes are then mounted in order: /api (auth), /api/topics (topics and comments), / (Pug pages), and finally express.static for assets in public/. Anything unmatched hits a 404 handler that returns JSON for API clients and a rendered 404.pug for browsers.

items.title: Code layout
items.text: Directory Responsibility routes/ Endpoint definitions, wire middleware to controllers controllers/ Request handling and response shaping models/ Data access for User and Topic over the pg pool middleware/ Security, sessions, rate limiting, validation, error handling utils/ Token helpers, validation rules, response envelope, logging db/ PostgreSQL pool (index.js) and schema (schema.sql) views/ Pug templates public/ Static CSS, JS and images seeds/ Demo-topic seeding

items.title: Startup sequence
items.text: On boot the app reads db/schema.sql and runs it against PostgreSQL, creating any missing tables. Only after the schema is ready does it optionally seed demo topics and start listening. If the database connection fails, the process exits with a non-zero code rather than serving requests against a broken backend.

items.title: Responses
items.text: Every controller returns through a shared response helper, so clients always get the same envelope: a success flag, statusCode, human-readable message, data, a timestamp, and a requestId. List endpoints add a pagination block. This consistency is what the API reference documents.

items.title: Auth model
items.text:
See also: API reference · Security

Kursforum combines stateless JWT access tokens with a server-side session and a failed-login lockout. This page explains why those pieces exist and how they interact.

items.title: Tokens
items.text:
Login and registration return a token pair:

  • An access token (default lifetime 15m) that the client sends on protected requests as Authorization: Bearer <token>.
  • A refresh token (default 7d) used only to obtain a new access token from POST /api/refresh.

Short-lived access tokens limit the damage if one leaks; the refresh token lets a client stay logged in without re-entering credentials. The two are signed with separate secrets (ACCESS_TOKEN_SECRET, REFRESH_TOKEN_SECRET) so a leaked access secret can't mint refresh tokens.

items.title: Why also a session
items.text: Alongside the tokens, login stores a session in PostgreSQL (connect-pg-simple, the session table). The session holds the user id, username and a sessionId, and logout destroys it. The session gives the server a place to invalidate a login, something a purely stateless JWT can't do on its own.

items.title: Protecting a route
items.text: The protect middleware guards every write endpoint. It expects Authorization: Bearer <accessToken>, verifies the signature and expiry, and attaches the decoded user to the request. Missing or invalid tokens are rejected before the controller runs, and the rejection is logged as a security event.

items.title: Account lockout
items.text:
Repeated bad passwords don't just fail, they count. The flow:

flowchart TD
    A["Login attempt"] --> B{Password correct?}
    B -- yes --> C["Reset failure counter, issue tokens"]
    B -- no --> D["Increment failed_login_attempts"]
    D --> E{"Reached MAX_FAILED_LOGIN_ATTEMPTS?"}
    E -- yes --> F["Set lockout_until, reject until it passes"]
    E -- no --> G["Reject with generic error"]

Defaults: lock after 5 failures (MAX_FAILED_LOGIN_ATTEMPTS), stay locked 15 minutes (LOCKOUT_DURATION_MINUTES), and reset the counter after 60 idle minutes (RESET_FAILURES_AFTER_MINUTES). A locked account is rejected even with the right password until the lock expires.

Login errors don't reveal whether a username exists

An unknown username and a wrong password return the same generic authentication error, so an attacker can't probe for valid usernames. A locked account returns a distinct "account locked" message. In every case the detailed reason is written only to the server logs.

items.title: Security
items.text:
See also: Auth model · Environment variables

Kursforum layers several defenses in front of the application logic. This page explains what each layer does and why it's there. Configuration values live in Environment variables.

items.title: Transport and headers
items.text:
• Helmet sets a baseline of secure HTTP response headers, plus a few custom headers on top.
  • HTTPS enforcement redirects HTTP to HTTPS when ENFORCE_HTTPS=true. It stays off for local development, where there's no TLS.

items.title: CORS
items.text: The allowed origins depend on NODE_ENV. In production the app reads ALLOWED_ORIGINS, a comma-separated allowlist, so only known front-ends can call the API from a browser. Development uses a looser config for convenience.

items.title: Input handling
items.text:
• Body size limit — JSON bodies are capped at 10kb, so a request can't exhaust memory.
  • Validation — express-validator chains in utils/validationRules.js check every field (lengths, allowed characters, the TIA/TIS/TIK course set) before a controller runs. Failures return a structured validation error.
  • HPP — HTTP Parameter Pollution protection strips duplicated query parameters that could otherwise bypass validation.

items.title: Rate limiting
items.text:
Per-IP limits throttle abuse. Auth endpoints are the strictest, because login and registration are the attack surface for credential stuffing.

Scope Default Why General API 100 / window Blunt brute-force against reads and writes Auth 5 / window Slow down credential stuffing on login/registration Refresh 10 / window Limit token-refresh abuse

Rate limiting is disabled when NODE_ENV=test so the test suite isn't throttled.

items.title: Accounts and secrets
items.text:
• Password hashing — passwords are stored as bcrypt hashes, never in plaintext.
  • Account lockout — repeated failed logins lock an account temporarily; see Auth model.
  • Split JWT secrets — access and refresh tokens use separate signing secrets.

Development secrets are not production secrets

The secrets in .env.example and docker-compose.yml are placeholders. A real deployment must set strong, unique values and serve over HTTPS with ENFORCE_HTTPS=true.

items.title: How-to guides
items.text:
Task-oriented recipes for running and operating Kursforum: starting it with or without Docker, seeding demo data, and running the test suite.

Browse the guides in the sidebar.

items.title: Run the tests
items.text: See also: Run with Docker · API reference

items.title: Overview
items.text: The test suite uses Mocha, Chai and Supertest to drive the Express app through its HTTP layer. Tests run with NODE_ENV=test, which disables rate limiting so requests aren't throttled during a run.

items.title: Run against a local database
items.text:
Requires a reachable PostgreSQL instance configured through your .env (see Run without Docker).

npm test

This runs mocha --recursive "tests" --extension test.js over every *.test.js file under tests/.

items.title: Run in Docker
items.text:
Builds the containers, starts them, and runs the suite inside the app container against the Compose PostgreSQL service:

npm run test:docker

Use this when you don't have a local PostgreSQL, or to match the CI-style environment exactly.

items.title: Verify
items.text: A passing run ends with Mocha's summary and a 0 exit code. A non-zero exit code means at least one test failed; the failing assertions are printed above the summary.

items.title: Run with Docker
items.text: See also: Quickstart · Environment variables

items.title: Overview
items.text: The Docker Compose setup runs the full stack: PostgreSQL, the Express app, and an Nginx reverse proxy. It bakes in development secrets and seeds demo topics, so it runs without a local .env file. Use it for local development and demos.

items.title: Prerequisites
items.text:
• Docker and Docker Compose
  • Ports 80, 3001, and 5432 free

items.title: Start the stack
items.text:
docker compose up --build

Startup order is enforced by healthchecks: postgres (checked with pg_isready) must be healthy before app starts, and app must pass its /health check before nginx starts.

The app is reachable at:

  • http://localhost — through Nginx (port 80)
  • http://localhost:3001 — the Express app directly

items.title: Stop the stack
items.text:
docker compose down

To also drop the database volume and start fresh next time:

docker compose down -v

down -v deletes all data

The -v flag removes the postgres-data volume, wiping every registered user, topic and comment. Leave it off to keep your data between runs.

items.title: Change configuration
items.text: The container environment is defined inline in docker-compose.yml under the app service. To change ports, secrets, seeding, or rate limits, edit those values there. For the meaning of each variable, see Environment variables.

items.title: Verify
items.text:
curl http://localhost/health

A 200 response with "status": "OK" means the app is up and connected to PostgreSQL.

items.title: Run without Docker
items.text: See also: Run with Docker · Environment variables

items.title: Overview
items.text: Run the Express app directly against a local PostgreSQL instance. Use this when you want fast restarts and a debugger attached, without rebuilding containers.

items.title: Prerequisites
items.text:
• Node.js and npm
  • A running PostgreSQL server with a database named kursforum
  • Node dependencies installed:
npm install

items.title: Step 1 — Create the environment file
items.text:
Copy the example and adjust the values for your local PostgreSQL:

cp .env.example .env

At minimum, set DATABASE_URL to point at your database:

DATABASE_URL=postgresql://postgres:postgres@127.0.0.1:5432/kursforum

The token and session secrets can keep their example values for local development. See Environment variables for every option.

items.title: Step 2 — Start the app
items.text:
npm start

On startup the app reads db/schema.sql and applies it, so the users, topics, comments and session tables are created automatically if they don't exist. It listens on PORT (default 3001).

items.title: Step 3 — Verify
items.text:
curl http://localhost:3001/health

A 200 response with "status": "OK" confirms the app started and reached PostgreSQL.

No Nginx in this mode

Running without Docker skips the Nginx reverse proxy. Reach the app directly on http://localhost:3001 instead of http://localhost.

items.title: Next steps
items.text:
• Load demo topics: Seed demo topics
  • Exercise the endpoints: API reference

items.title: Seed demo topics
items.text: See also: Environment variables · Database schema

items.title: Overview
items.text: The app can insert demo topics into PostgreSQL on startup so the forum isn't empty. Seeding is controlled by three environment variables and reads topics from a JSON file. This runs after the schema is applied, from seeds/seedTopics.js.

items.title: Prerequisites
items.text:
• A configured environment (.env for local, or the inline env in docker-compose.yml for Docker)
  • A seed file in the expected format (a default lives at seeds/topics.seed.json)

items.title: Step 1 — Turn seeding on
items.text:
Set the seeding variables:

Variable Value Effect SEED_TOPICS true Enable seeding on startup SEED_TOPICS_ON_EMPTY_ONLY true Seed only when the topics table is empty SEED_TOPICS_FILE path Seed file to read (default seeds/topics.seed.json)

Set SEED_TOPICS_ON_EMPTY_ONLY=false to re-seed on every startup. Topics are keyed by seed_key (a unique column), so re-runs update matching rows instead of creating duplicates.

items.title: Step 2 — Provide the seed data
items.text:
Each entry in the JSON array describes one topic:

[
  {
    "seedKey": "welcome-tia",
    "title": "Willkommen im TIA-Forum",
    "content": "Stellt euch hier kurz vor.",
    "kurs": "TIA",
    "seedAuthorName": "Amica",
    "seedComment": { "content": "Viel Erfolg!", "seedAuthorName": "Tamara" }
  }
]

Required per topic: seedKey, title, content, kurs (one of TIA, TIS, TIK). Optional: seedAuthorName and a single seedComment.

items.title: Step 3 — Start the app
items.text:
Seeding runs automatically on the next startup:

npm start

The Docker setup already ships SEED_TOPICS=true with SEED_TOPICS_ON_EMPTY_ONLY=false, so demo topics appear on every docker compose up.

items.title: Verify
items.text:
curl "http://localhost:3001/api/topics"

The response lists the seeded topics. Seeded rows have a seedAuthorName and no linked user account.

items.title: Reference
items.text:
The exact facts about Kursforum: the HTTP API, every environment variable, and the database schema. Values here are read from the source, not summarized from memory.

Browse the reference in the sidebar.

items.title: HTTP API
items.text:
See also: Auth model · Environment variables · Database schema

Every JSON endpoint the app exposes, its inputs, and its responses. Page routes that render HTML (/, /register, /forum) are listed under Pages.

items.title: Conventions
items.text:
• Base path for the API is /api. Through Nginx the app is at http://localhost; direct, it's http://localhost:3001.
  • All API requests and responses are JSON. The request body limit is 10kb.
  • Protected endpoints require an access token in the Authorization header: Authorization: Bearer <accessToken>.
  • Rate limits apply per IP and are disabled when NODE_ENV=test. See Rate limiting.

items.title: Success envelope
items.text:
Every successful response uses this shape:

{
  "success": true,
  "statusCode": 200,
  "message": "Erfolg",
  "data": {},
  "timestamp": "2026-01-01T00:00:00.000Z",
  "requestId": "uuid"
}

List endpoints add a pagination object alongside data.

items.title: Error envelope
items.text:
{
  "success": false,
  "statusCode": 400,
  "message": "Validierungsfehler",
  "errorCode": "VALIDATION_ERROR",
  "errorId": "uuid",
  "timestamp": "2026-01-01T00:00:00.000Z",
  "requestId": "uuid"
}

Field-level details are included only when NODE_ENV=development.

items.title: Health
items.text:
Method Path Auth Purpose GET /health none Liveness probe used by Docker healthchecks

Response data (returned at the top level, not wrapped):

{ "status": "OK", "timestamp": "2026-01-01T00:00:00.000Z", "uptime": 12.34 }

items.title: Auth
items.text:
Defined in routes/authRoutes.js.

Method Path Auth Purpose POST /api/registration none Register a new user POST /api/login none Authenticate and receive tokens POST /api/refresh none Exchange a refresh token for a new access token POST /api/logout Bearer Invalidate the current session

items.title: POST /api/registration
items.text:
Request body:

Field Rules firstname 1–50 chars, letters/spaces/apostrophes/hyphens lastname 1–50 chars, letters/spaces/apostrophes/hyphens username 3–20 chars, letters/numbers/underscores/hyphens; stored lowercase password min 8 chars, must include an uppercase letter, a lowercase letter and a number course one of TIA, TIS, TIK

Returns 201 with the created user plus an access token, refresh token, sessionId, and expiresIn. Returns 409 (ConflictError) if the username is taken.

items.title: POST /api/login
items.text:
Request body: username, password.

Returns 200 with the user and a fresh token pair. Returns 401 for an unknown user, a wrong password, or a locked account. Repeated failures lock the account, see Auth model.

items.title: POST /api/refresh
items.text:
Request body: refreshToken.

Returns 200 with a new accessToken and expiresIn: "15m". Returns 401 if the refresh token is missing, invalid, or its user no longer exists.

items.title: POST /api/logout
items.text: Requires Authorization: Bearer <accessToken>. Destroys the server-side session and returns 200.

items.title: Topics
items.text:
Defined in routes/topicRoutes.js.

Method Path Auth Purpose GET /api/topics none List topics, filtered and paginated POST /api/topics Bearer Create a topic DELETE /api/topics/:id Bearer Delete a topic (author only) POST /api/topics/:id/comments Bearer Add a comment to a topic

items.title: GET /api/topics
items.text:
Query parameters:

Parameter Default Notes kurs — Filter by course; ignored unless it's TIA, TIS or TIK page 1 Page number, clamped to >= 1 limit 10 Items per page, clamped to 1–100

Returns 200 with a paginated list. Each topic carries a resolved authorName (the registered user's username, else the seed author name, else Unbekannt). The pagination object:

{
  "total": 42,
  "page": 1,
  "limit": 10,
  "totalPages": 5,
  "hasNextPage": true,
  "hasPrevPage": false
}

items.title: POST /api/topics
items.text:
Request body:

Field Rules title 5–100 chars content 10–10000 chars kurs one of TIA, TIS, TIK

Returns 201 with the created topic. The author is taken from the access token, not the body.

items.title: DELETE /api/topics/:id
items.text: Deletes the topic identified by :id. Returns 200 on success. Returns an authorization error if the caller is not the topic's author; deleting another user's topic is rejected.

items.title: POST /api/topics/:id/comments
items.text:
Request body: content (1–1000 chars).

Returns 201 with the topic including the newly added comment and its resolved author.

items.title: Pages
items.text:
Defined in routes/pageRoutes.js. These render Pug templates and return HTML, not JSON.

Method Path Template Purpose GET / index.pug Landing page GET /register registration.pug Registration form GET /forum forumpage.pug Forum view

Unknown paths return a JSON 404 for API/non-HTML clients, or the rendered 404.pug page for browsers.

items.title: Database schema
items.text:
See also: Architecture · API reference

The PostgreSQL schema in db/schema.sql. The app applies it on startup with CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS, so tables are created automatically if they don't exist.

items.title: users
items.text:
Registered accounts, including login-security bookkeeping.

Column Type Notes id SERIAL Primary key firstname VARCHAR(50) Not null lastname VARCHAR(50) Not null username VARCHAR(20) Unique, not null, stored lowercase password VARCHAR(255) Not null, bcrypt hash course VARCHAR(3) Not null, one of TIA, TIS, TIK (CHECK) failed_login_attempts INTEGER Not null, default 0 lockout_until TIMESTAMPTZ Set while an account is locked last_failed_login_at TIMESTAMPTZ last_login_at TIMESTAMPTZ created_at TIMESTAMPTZ Not null, default NOW() updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ Not null, default NOW()

items.title: topics
items.text:
Forum topics, one per thread.

Column Type Notes id SERIAL Primary key title TEXT Not null content TEXT Not null kurs VARCHAR(3) Not null, one of TIA, TIS, TIK (CHECK) seed_key VARCHAR(100) Unique; identifies seeded rows seed_author_name VARCHAR(50) Display author for seeded topics author_id INTEGER References users(id), ON DELETE SET NULL created_at TIMESTAMPTZ Not null, default NOW()

items.title: comments
items.text:
Comments attached to a topic.

Column Type Notes id SERIAL Primary key topic_id INTEGER Not null, references topics(id), ON DELETE CASCADE content TEXT Not null author_id INTEGER References users(id), ON DELETE SET NULL seed_author_name VARCHAR(50) Display author for seeded comments created_at TIMESTAMPTZ Not null, default NOW()

items.title: session
items.text:
Server-side session store for connect-pg-simple.

Column Type Notes sid varchar Primary key sess json Session payload expire timestamp(6) Not null; indexed by IDX_session_expire

items.title: Relationships
items.text:
erDiagram
    users ||--o{ topics : "author_id"
    users ||--o{ comments : "author_id"
    topics ||--o{ comments : "topic_id"

Deleting a user sets author_id to NULL on their topics and comments (the content stays). Deleting a topic cascades to its comments.

items.title: Environment variables
items.text:
See also: Run without Docker · Security

Every variable the app reads at runtime, its default, and where it matters. For local runs these live in .env (copy from .env.example); under Docker they are set inline in docker-compose.yml.

items.title: Server
items.text: Variable Default Description NODE_ENV development development, production, or test. Controls CORS config, error detail, and rate limiting. PORT 3001 HTTP listen port for the Express app.

items.title: Database
items.text: Variable Default Description DATABASE_URL — PostgreSQL connection string, postgresql://USER:PASSWORD@HOST:PORT/DBNAME. Required. DATABASE_SSL false Set to true if the server requires SSL. When true, the client connects with rejectUnauthorized: false.

items.title: Tokens and sessions
items.text:
Variable Default Description ACCESS_TOKEN_SECRET — Signing secret for JWT access tokens. REFRESH_TOKEN_SECRET — Signing secret for JWT refresh tokens. SESSION_SECRET — Secret for the Express session cookie. ACCESS_TOKEN_EXPIRY 15m Access token lifetime. REFRESH_TOKEN_EXPIRY 7d Refresh token lifetime.

Change the secrets outside local development

The example and Docker values are throwaway development secrets. Any real deployment must set strong, unique values for all three secrets.

items.title: Logging
items.text: Variable Default Description LOG_LEVEL info Winston log level.

items.title: Security
items.text: Variable Default Description ALLOWED_ORIGINS — Comma-separated CORS allowlist, used when NODE_ENV=production. ENFORCE_HTTPS false Redirect HTTP to HTTPS. Must be false for local development.

items.title: Rate limiting
items.text:
Requests per window, per IP. Bypassed entirely when NODE_ENV=test.

Variable Default Applies to GENERAL_RATE_LIMIT 100 General API routes (topics, comments) AUTH_RATE_LIMIT 5 Login and registration REFRESH_RATE_LIMIT 10 Token refresh

items.title: Account lockout
items.text: Variable Default Description MAX_FAILED_LOGIN_ATTEMPTS 5 Failed logins before the account locks. LOCKOUT_DURATION_MINUTES 15 How long an account stays locked. RESET_FAILURES_AFTER_MINUTES 60 Idle window after which the failure counter resets.

items.title: Demo data seeding
items.text: Variable Default Description SEED_TOPICS false Seed demo topics on startup. SEED_TOPICS_ON_EMPTY_ONLY true Only seed when the topics table is empty. SEED_TOPICS_FILE seeds/topics.seed.json Path to the seed file.

items.title: Tutorials
items.text:
Learning-oriented guides that take you from an empty checkout to a running Kursforum. Start with the Quickstart.

Browse the tutorials in the sidebar.

items.title: Quickstart
items.text:
See also: Run with Docker · Architecture

Get the full Kursforum stack, PostgreSQL, the Express app and Nginx, running locally and open the forum in your browser. About five minutes.

items.title: Prerequisites
items.text:
• Docker and Docker Compose
  • Git
  • A free port 80 (Nginx) and 3001 (app) and 5432 (PostgreSQL) on your machine

items.title: 1. Get the code
items.text:
git clone https://github.com/Just-Martin-Really/Web-Engineering-Semester1.git
cd Web-Engineering-Semester1

items.title: 2. Start the stack
items.text:
The Docker Compose setup already ships development secrets and turns on demo-data seeding, so no .env file is needed for a first run.

docker compose up --build

Compose starts three containers in order: postgres becomes healthy first, then app connects and creates the schema, then nginx comes up as the reverse proxy.

items.title: 3. Open the forum
items.text:
Once the logs show PostgreSQL connected and schema ready, open:

  • http://localhost — the landing page, served through Nginx

From there:

  • /register — create an account (pick course TIA, TIS or TIK)
  • /forum — read topics and post comments

The database is seeded with demo topics on startup (SEED_TOPICS=true in docker-compose.yml), so the forum is not empty on first launch.

items.title: 4. Check the health endpoint
items.text:
curl http://localhost/health

{ "status": "OK", "timestamp": "2026-01-01T00:00:00.000Z", "uptime": 12.34 }

This is the same endpoint the Docker healthchecks poll.

items.title: What you have now
items.text:
• A running forum at http://localhost
  • A PostgreSQL database with the schema applied and demo topics seeded
  • A JSON API under /api (see the API reference)

items.title: Next steps
items.text:
• Post and comment through the API: API reference
  • Run the stack without Docker: Run without Docker
  • Understand how requests flow through the system: Architecture
